text: So I do a bunch of videos that are fun or interesting. This video is a little different in the sense that I think this is a really important video. One issue that comes up all the time as I go out into the community and proctor new robotic surgeons or surgeons come and visit us at our mentorship site is what if I get into bleeding while I'm doing a robotic lung resection? You know, oh my God, I feel like I'm so far away from that patient asleep. on the operating room table. And the reality is if you do enough of these robotic lung resections, you are going to get into bleeding once in a while. It shouldn't happen often. I quote patients about one percent of the time. I do about a hundred cases a year. So it happens about once a year. And I'm going to show some actual video from my last two conversions for bleeding, which were fourteen months apart. And both patients did fine. Neither one required a blood transfusion. But There's a sequence of steps that I think you should be familiar with. I think your team should be familiar with. And you should probably do fire drills like we do at our hospital intermittently so everybody in the team understands here's what happens if we get into bleeding and we have to convert to an open case. It should be done carefully, safely,

The surgeon encounters bleeding during a left-sided pulmonary lobectomy. The medial robotic arm, equipped with a Cartier Arm One, applies pressure with a sponge to the bleeding site, focusing on the pulmonary artery and vein. The camera is repositioned through the assistant port for continuous visualization of the bleeder. Instruments are removed to prepare for conversion to a posterolateral thoracotomy, allowing manual control of the bleeding. The robotic arm holding pressure is withdrawn, and the robot is moved out slowly and controlled.

I'm trying to think if there was anything else I needed to tell them. Oh, so let's say you did grab. With your medial arm, you grab the bleeder. Instead of holding pressure, you actually have it grab. Well, the thing you want to remember is you can't just slide the instrument out because you'll tear the bleeder. So there's an emergency release, emergency instrument release key. I think Chanel holds it up there. It has to be on your cart. Everybody who does robotic thoracic surgery should have that key. Or you can have a second surgeon go to the console and release it. But either way, you know, the key will release it. But I prefer just to apply pressure with the medial arm. And that way you can just slide it off. And we do this fire drill every once in a while here. So everybody in the room knows exactly what to do. And the conversions always go really smoothly.

During the left lower lobectomy, the surgeon encounters a positive hilar node and performs a dissection with caution. A spondylole is positioned above the dissection site to hold pressure in case of bleeding. As bleeding occurs, the surgeon uses the spondylole to apply pressure to the pulmonary artery, a low-pressure system, while the camera captures the spurt of blood. The camera is then removed for cleaning.

The surgeon uses a suction device to remove blood from the surgical field while maintaining pressure on the bleeder to control the bleeding from the pulmonary artery. The robotic arms remain in place as the surgeon steps away from the console to assess the situation and communicate with the surgical team. After holding pressure for three minutes, the surgeon evaluates the bleeding control, considering whether to continue the procedure or convert to an open thoracotomy.

The surgeon discusses the approach to controlling bleeding during a pulmonary lobectomy, emphasizing the use of the medial arm to hold pressure on the bleeder. For a left-sided resection, the medial arm is identified as Arm One, while for a right-sided resection, it would be Arm Four. The surgeon plans to convert to a posterolateral thoracotomy, removing the lateral arms, including the camera port, to safely manage the bleeding.

The surgeon holds pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm, while the camera is repositioned from arm two to the assistant port for continuous visualization of the sponge stick on the bleeder. The lateral three robotic ports are removed to facilitate the conversion to a posterolateral thoracotomy.

After completing the thoracotomy, the surgeon manually controls the bleeding through the thoracotomy incision. Arm one of the robotic system is removed, allowing the entire robot to be moved out of the surgical field for a safe conversion.

The surgeon attempts an anteromedial basilar segmentectomy on the left lower lobe, working on small pulmonary artery branches in a tight space. A sponge roll is visible in the chest, ready to hold pressure in case of bleeding. As the robotic stapler is fired across the space, bleeding occurs. The surgeon holds pressure with the sponge roll without grabbing the bleeder, maintaining control in the low-pressure system. The assistant uses a suction device to manage the bleeding, while preparations for thoracotomy conversion are underway.

The surgeon maintains pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm, while the assistant removes the camera from arm two and places it through the assistant port for continuous visualization. The lateral robotic ports are removed, and the surgeon scrubs in preparation for a posterolateral thoracotomy through the fifth intercostal space. The robotic arm holding pressure is slid out, and the robot is moved aside to allow manual control of the bleeder.

And I'll control the bleeder with a stitch or whatever needs to be done through the thoracotomy. And these are the steps of a safe conversion. So the main takeaway point is that conversion to thoracotomy for bleeding during robotic lung resection can be done safely, should be done safely. It's just a sequence of steps. You have to have a plan like anything else in surgery. You need to be prepared. The appropriate equipment is in the room. The team should be familiar with these steps. You should practice them with fire drills as necessary. But having been through a few of these now, I can tell you that I believe in my heart that robotic surgery, is the best way to do major lung resection, and it is the safest way to do major lung resection. The patient is never in any danger as long as you have a plan and you're prepared. Again, I appreciate you watching these videos. I look forward to your comments. You can always get a hold of me. My contact information is available through my website, roboticthoracic.surgery, and I wish you good luck, and I look forward to hearing from you. Thanks.
